t: 5.1 Core symptoms (DSM-5-TR — ≥ 5 symptoms × 2 weeks, at least 1 of which is 1 or 2)
Anhedonia — loss of interest and pleasure.
Psychomotor agitation or retardation (observable by others).
6.1 Unified diagnostic criteria (DSM-5-TR · ICD-11 consensus points)
The presence of 5 or more symptoms for ≥ 2 weeks; at least one of which must be depressed mood or anhedonia.
Exclusion of substance or medical condition.
Never had a manic or hypomanic episode.
"Bereavement exclusion" removed (in DSM-IV, diagnosis was not made during 2 months of bereavement); grief and depressive episode can co-occur.
single episode separately coded; severity and psychotic feature qualifiers.
Severity classification: mild, severe (instead of mild/moderate/severe in DSM); treatment algorithms based on severity.
Scales - PHQ-9 (screening and monitoring), HAM-D, MADRS, BDI-II.
Bipolar screening (MDQ, HCL-32) — to rule out bipolar episodes.
Medical and laboratory (thyroid, B12, folate, ferritin, liver, kidney, pregnancy) — to exclude somatic causes.
History of manic or hypomanic episode.
Response to stress factor; fewer criteria.
After loss of a loved one; 'wave-like' course; self-esteem is preserved.
PHQ-9 — standard in primary care; ≥10 score indicates moderate-severe depression.
Laboratory: complete blood count, thyroid, B12, folate, ferritin, liver/kidney, glucose.
EKG — before starting TCA or QT-prolonging medications.
8.1 General Principles (NICE NG222 · APA 2019 · CANMAT 2016 Consensus)
Mild (PHQ-9 5–9) — guided self-help, CBT-based internet interventions, physical activity, observation.
Moderate (PHQ-9 10–14) — CBT or IPT, or pharmacotherapy (SSRI); patient choice.
Severe (PHQ-9 ≥ 20 or psychotic features) — combination of pharmacotherapy + psychotherapy; severe psychotic/suicidal risk — inpatient admission and ECT.
— sertraline, escitalopram, fluoxetine, paroxetine, citalopram. Effect in 4–6 weeks; titration required.
— venlafaxine, duloxetine — alternative or comorbid pain/discomfort.
mirtazapine (sleep and appetite problems), bupropion (few sexual side effects, ADHD comorbid), agomelatine, vortioxetine (cognitive component).
SSRI + lithium or quetiapine or aripiprazole; SSRI combinations; ketamine/esketamine (intranasal — FDA approved 2019, severe refractory); ECT.

— monotherapy in mild-to-moderate depression, combination with pharmacotherapy in severe cases; specific methods and evidence base in the "Treatment Guidelines" list (CBT, IPT, behavioral activation, MBCT).
Active assessment; hospitalization if risk high; restricted access to means (weapons, medications); psychosocial support.
first episode: continuation after remission for 6–12 months; long-term in case of relapse.
First-line; sexual side effect; SIADH (in elderly); citalopram > 40 mg QT prolongation.
Mirtazapine: sleep and appetite; bupropion: no sexual side effects.
Second-third order; cardiac and anticholinergic side effects; overdose lethal.
FDA 2019, refractory; under supervision in clinic.
stepwise algorithm based on severity; "less severe" — CBT or guided self-help first-line; "more severe" — combination.
SSRI first-line; CBT / IPT first-line alternative.
A 4-step algorithm based on severity and incident.
Cipriani A. et al. Lancet 2018 network meta-analiz:

Comparison of 21 antidepressants — agomelatine and amitriptyline highest efficacy; fluoxetine and reboxetine lowest; fluoxetine and reboxetine highest in acceptability. Efficacy differences are clinically modest.

Cognitive restructuring, behavioral activation; 12–20 sessions. Evidence: Cuijpers P. et al. Cochrane meta-analyses — medium effect, comparable to pharmacotherapy; combination superior to monotherapy in severe cases.
Interpersonal Therapy (IPT — Interpersonal Therapy) — Klerman (Klerman G.), Weissman (Weissman M.)
4 problem areas – grief, role transition, role conflict, interpersonal deficit. 12–16 sessions. RCT evidence comparable to CBT.
Behavioral Activation — Jacobson N., Martell C.
— Systematic increase of positive activities, reduction of avoidance. Dimidjian S. et al. J Consult Clin Psychol 2006 RCT — comparable effect to CBT.
Mindfulness-Based Cognitive Therapy (MBCT) — Segal Z., Williams M., Teasdale J.
In relapse prevention; particularly if history of ≥3 episodes, effect may be superior to antidepressant continuation (Kuyken W. et al. Lancet 2015).
Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) — Kroenke (Kroenke K.), Spitzer (Spitzer R.L.)
— Gold standard screening and monitoring tool in primary medical care.
Severe psychotic depression, refractory, suicide risk, food intake disorder, pregnancy. UK ECT Review Group Lancet 2003 — ECT superior to pharmacotherapy in severe cases.
— FDA 2019 intranasal esketamine (Spravato) for severe refractory depression; rapid antidepressant effect (hours-days); administration under clinic supervision. Daly E.J. et al. JAMA Psychiatry 2018.
